title: Pattern Matching in Rust
content:
### Pattern Matching in Rust

Pattern matching is a fundamental feature in Rust used with `match`, `if let`, `while let`, and `let` destructuring.

---

### Pattern Types

| Pattern | Example | Description |
|---------|---------|-------------|
| Literal | `1` | Match a specific value |
| Variable | `x` | Binds to any value |
| Wildcard | `_` | Matches any value, ignores it |
| Range | `1..=5` | Matches values in range |
| Or | `a | b` | Matches a or b |
| Struct | `Point { x, y }` | Destructures a struct |
| Tuple | `(a, b, c)` | Destructures a tuple |
| Array | `[a, .., b]` | Matches array with rest |
| Guard | `x if x > 0` | Additional condition |

---

### Advanced Pattern Examples

```rust
// Destructuring structs
let p = Point { x: 0, y: 7 };
let Point { x: a, y: b } = p;

// Destructuring with match guards
match pair {
    (x, y) if x == y => println!("Equal"),
    (x, _) if x % 2 == 0 => println!("Even x"),
    _ => println!("No match"),
}

// @ bindings
match id {
    e @ 0..=9 => println!("Single digit: {}", e),
    _ => println!("Other"),
}
```

> **Key Point:** Patterns in Rust are irrefutable (always match, like in `let`) or refutable (can fail, like in `match`). Use irrefutable patterns in `let` bindings and refutable patterns in `match` arms.

quiz.question: Which pattern matches any value but ignores it?
